title: Chat uses PSS for messages and GSOC for notifications, matching latency to purpose.
content:
## Decision

Connect's chat/collab stack uses two complementary Swarm protocols, each selected for the traffic it serves:

| Feature | Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Chat messages | **PSS** | End-to-end encrypted, 1-to-1, async delivery acceptable |
| Typing indicators | **GSOC** | Low latency (<1s), ephemeral |
| Document share notification | **GSOC** | Instant ping when share bundle is ready |
| Live collaboration signals | **GSOC** | Sub-second cursor/op-committed |
| Operation sync | **Feeds + GSOC notify** | Persistent ops via feeds, GSOC triggers pull |

## Rationale

- PSS gives per-message asymmetric encryption for free but costs 2–10s of Trojan mining. That's fine for a chat message, fatal for a typing hint.
- GSOC mines the signer once (~10–30s) and then every subsequent send is a normal SOC update — milliseconds of latency, symmetric encryption via the shared signer.
- The two protocols compose: PSS carries the message, GSOC pings the recipient's UI to render it immediately instead of waiting for the next PSS poll.

## Consequence

Typing indicators were designed on top of GSOC but ultimately scratched. The GSOC plumbing survives and is being reused for live collaboration.
description: PSS's per-message asymmetric encryption fits private 1-to-1 chat; GSOC's sub-second symmetric SOC pings fit ephemeral presence, typing, and doc-update signals.
